mnemonics: Breadth-First, Depth-First, Uniform-Cost, D-I-D-S, Bidirectional. Remember: Big Dogs Dig Under Bridges (Breadth, Depth, Uniform, D-I-D-S, Bidirectional)
keyPoints: Uninformed search strategies have no additional information about the goal other than its definition.
Breadth-First Search (BFS) expands the shallowest node first and is complete and optimal for unit step cost.
Depth-First Search (DFS) expands the deepest node first and is not complete in infinite spaces nor optimal.
Uniform-Cost Search expands the node with the lowest path cost and is both complete and optimal.
Depth-Limited and Iterative Deepening Search (D-I-D-S) are variations to handle DFS's limitations in infinite spaces.
commonMistakes: Assuming DFS is always more memory-efficient than BFS; while DFS has a lower memory requirement for the fringe, BFS can be more efficient in terms of total nodes explored for certain problems.
Confusing completeness with optimality; a strategy can be complete (will find a solution if one exists) but not optimal (the first solution found may not be the best one).
Forgetting that Uniform-Cost Search is a more general form of BFS, where BFS is optimal only when all step costs are equal.
